properties.Narrative: This project aerially seeded treated pinyon–juniper habitat following mastication to promote the recovery of native grasses and forbs on public lands. properties.Project Name: Dry Creek Guzzler Repair and Fencing

properties.Project Name: Sudduth and Williams Draws Fence Replacement
properties.Public Name: Sudduth and Williams Draws Fence Replacement
properties.Narrative: This project improved habitat connectivity by replacing hazardous range fencing with new wildlife-friendly fencing on public lands. The work reduces the risk of wildlife injury and allows mule deer and other wildlife to move more freely across the landscape.

properties.Project Name: Little Fish Lake Valley Habitat Improvement Project Phase 1

properties.Project Name: Improving Big Game Health: A Fence Retrofitting Program

This project entailed installing a 10,000-gallon water storage tank and 3,000 feet of waterline to supply water to two new troughs. In addition to providing livestock and wildlife with valuable watering sources in this desert ecosystem, this development will improve livestock distribution, thereby enhancing forage diversification and quality.

This project improved mule deer habitat on private lands through hand cut-and-pile treatments that reduced dense vegetation and lowered catastrophic wildfire risk near the Uinta-Wasatch-Cache National Forest. Creating a more diverse mosaic of habitat conditions improved forage availability, cover, and movement opportunities for mule deer and other wildlife.

properties.Project Name: Eagle Mountain Mule Deer Migration Corridor Preservation
properties.Public Name: Eagle Mountain Mule Deer Migration Corridor Preservation
properties.Narrative: This project improved mule deer migration habitat by supporting wildlife-friendly fencing and protecting important open space within a key movement corridor. The work helps maintain safe wildlife passage and long-term connectivity across the landscape.

Targeted weed spraying and hand treatments remove invasive plants that outcompete native shrubs and forbs, allowing high-quality forage species preferred by mule deer to reestablish and thrive. Restoring these native plant communities improves habitat productivity, enhances browse availability, and supports healthier deer populations while reducing fire risk and improving ecosystem resilience.

This project restored declining aspen habitat within the well-known Aspen Alley area of the Sierra Madre Mountains through mastication of diseased and dying trees. The treatments improved habitat conditions, stimulated new vegetation growth, and enhanced both mule deer habitat and recreational value across the landscape.

This project repaired critical wildlife guzzlers on public lands to provide reliable water sources before winter conditions limited access to the area. MDF staff restored multiple guzzlers that support mule deer, pronghorn, elk, sage grouse, and other wildlife, with additional repairs planned to continue improving habitat functionality across the landscape.

properties.Project Name: Three Affiliated Tribes National Park - Fence Removal
properties.Public Name: Three Affiliated Tribes National Park - Fence Removal
properties.Narrative: As a joint effort between the Mandan, Hidatsa and Arikara Nation, Mule Deer Foundation, North Dakota Wildlife Federation, Badlands Conservation Alliance, and Bobcat Company, this project entailed removing degraded and obsolete fencing on the Three Affiliated Tribes National Park. As a result, big game species such as mule deer, elk, and pronghorn will be able to move more safely into and out of the park, reducing habitat fragmentation within the Little Missouri River corridor.

This research project supported the capture and satellite radio-collaring of mule deer, white-tailed deer, and elk to improve understanding of movement, habitat use, and survival. By deploying new collars across multiple herds, the work generated critical data to inform science-based management, migration conservation, and long-term wildlife stewardship.

Following the 2018 Dog Creek Fire, this project planted 174,000 shrubs in 2019 and 2020 in collaboration with the Idaho Department of Fish and Game and the Bureau of Land Management to rehabilitate burned rangelands. The plantings restored sagebrush-dominated habitat, improved sagebrush ecosystem health, and provided essential winter forage for mule deer and other wildlife, supporting long-term range recovery and resilience.
properties.Partner Name: Idaho Department of Fish and Game

properties.Narrative: The first phase of the Burnt Beaver Project implemented mechanical thinning and hand cutting to create shaded fuel breaks and prepare the area for future prescribed burns. These treatments improve forest structure and resilience, supporting healthier vegetation and better habitat for mule deer.

properties.Project Name: West Escudilla PJ Restoration Project (Phases 1 and 2)
properties.Public Name: West Escudilla PJ Restoration Project (Phases 1 and 2)
properties.Narrative: Since 2019, this ongoing partnership has worked to reduce dense pinyon–juniper stands and restore historic grassland and savanna habitat on public lands. Removing excess trees allows native grasses and forbs to recover, improving forage availability, seasonal range, and overall habitat conditions for mule deer and other wildlife while also increasing landscape resilience to wildfire.

This project constructed Idaho's first wildlife overpass and guiding fencing along Idaho Highway 21 at Cervidae Peak to safely direct migrating animals across the roadway. With approximately 7,000 mule deer and 4,000 elk migrating through this corridor twice annually, the overpass and associated fencing reduced wildlife–vehicle collisions and improved connectivity between seasonal ranges, benefiting both wildlife and public safety.

This project treated invasive cheatgrass on private lands using aerial herbicide applications to improve rangeland health and reduce wildfire risk. Reducing invasive annual grasses increased the quality and availability of native forage for mule deer and other big game species while supporting long-term habitat resilience.

properties.Project Name: Lemhi Funnel Fence and Big Game Migration
properties.Public Name: Lemhi Funnel Fence and Big Game Migration
properties.Narrative: This project expanded wildlife funnel fencing to guide mule deer and other wildlife toward existing highway underpasses in a known wildlife–vehicle collision area. The work improves habitat connectivity, reduces collision risk, and creates safer travel corridors for both wildlife and motorists.

This project improved wildlife movement on private lands through a partnership with the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service and the Idaho Department of Fish and Game by removing, modifying, and replacing non-wildlife-friendly fencing. The fencing improvements reduced movement barriers and improved habitat connectivity for mule deer and other wildlife while supporting continued agricultural use of the land.

This project removed woven-wire fencing and replaced it with wildlife-friendly fencing to reduce barriers to movement and improve safety for mule deer and other wildlife. The new fencing improved habitat connectivity while continuing to support livestock management on the landscape.
properties.Partner Name: Idaho Department of Fish and Game

properties.Narrative: This project restored burned areas within an active mule deer migration corridor by planting native sagebrush to improve winter forage and habitat conditions. The plantings enhanced critical winter range for mule deer and elk while supporting long-term recovery and resilience of the post-fire landscape.
properties.Partner Name: Idaho Department of Fish and Game

properties.Narrative: This project is delivering dedicated wildlife crossings at Rocky Point along U.S. Highway 30 to protect a major mule deer migration route south of Montpelier. Project planning began in 2020, with construction starting in 2025 through a long-standing partnership between the Idaho Transportation Department and the Idaho Department of Fish and Game. By guiding migrating mule deer safely under the highway using purpose-built underpasses and wildlife fencing, the project reduces collisions in one of Idaho’s most dangerous roadway segments for wildlife while preserving connectivity between critical summer and winter habitats across the Bear Lake Plateau migration corridor.

In areas previously clear-cut and regrown with dense young conifers, crews used hand cut-and-pile thinning to open up the forest. This reduces overcrowding and enhances plant diversity, providing improved forage and cover for mule deer.

properties.Narrative: This project completed targeted timber stand improvement (TSI) treatments to release key browse species near the 10X Campground. Reducing competing trees allows shrubs and native vegetation to grow more vigorously, increasing the availability of high-quality forage for mule deer and other wildlife. These treatments improve habitat conditions while maintaining a healthy, balanced forest structure that benefits both wildlife and recreational users.

This project improved forest health by thinning small-diameter conifers and masticating overgrown brush to reduce competition among trees and stimulate new understory growth. The treatment promoted the resprouting of shrubs and forbs that provide valuable forage for mule deer, while preserving oak trees that produce important mast and wildlife habitat.

This project completed archaeological and environmental surveys needed to guide future habitat restoration work on public lands. The planning process helps ensure upcoming treatments can improve mule deer habitat and forest health while protecting important cultural resources and sensitive wildlife areas.

This project began a multi-phase effort to create shaded fuel breaks and improve wildlife habitat on public lands. Reducing dense vegetation helped create more wildfire-resilient landscapes while stimulating new forage growth and increasing acorn production that benefits mule deer, other wildlife, and nearby communities.

This project removed fire-damaged trees and excess woody material from public lands following the 2015 King Fire to reduce hazardous fuels and support long-term forest recovery. The remaining material was piled for future burning, and the completed treatment prepared the landscape for reforestation and the restoration of healthier wildlife habitat.

properties.Project Name: Arnold/Avery Phase 1 Mastication & Hazard Tree Felling
properties.Public Name: Arnold/Avery Phase 1 Mastication & Hazard Tree Felling
properties.Narrative: This project created shaded fuel breaks on public lands to reduce wildfire risk and improve protection for nearby communities. Thinning dense vegetation reduced competition among trees, increased sunlight reaching the forest floor, and stimulated new growth of grasses, forbs, brush, and acorn-producing oaks that provide valuable forage for mule deer and other wildlife.

This project rebuilt an important campground on public lands that provides access to hunting, fishing, and outdoor recreation opportunities. The work improves recreational infrastructure, supports local forest use, and helps connect the public to healthy wildlife habitat and conservation landscapes.

This project restored shrub-steppe habitat in the 2015 Soda Fire area by planting more than 130,000 native sagebrush seedlings across state and Bureau of Land Management lands. Reestablishing sagebrush-dominated communities improved ecological function and provided essential winter forage for mule deer, supporting long-term recovery of this fire-impacted landscape.

This project rehabilitated shrub-steppe habitat following the 2020 Badger Fire by planting approximately 200,000 native shrubs, including bitterbrush and sagebrush. Restoring sagebrush-dominated plant communities improved ecological function and provided essential winter forage for mule deer in this fire-affected landscape.
properties.Partner Name: Idaho Department of Fish and Game

properties.Narrative: This project removed and replaced dilapidated fencing with wildlife-friendly designs in partnership with the Idaho Department of Fish and Game. The improved fencing reduced the risk of injury or mortality while easing movement across the landscape, helping mule deer conserve energy as they travel between seasonal habitats.
properties.Partner Name: Idaho Department of Fish and Game

properties.Narrative: This project removed hazardous fencing that restricted wildlife movement and posed entanglement risks to mule deer and other wildlife. Removing these barriers improved habitat connectivity and allowed safer movement across the landscape.
properties.Partner Name: Idaho Department of Fish and Game

properties.Narrative: This project secured land for long-term conservation to protect important wildlife habitat and public values. The acquisition supported habitat connectivity, recreational access, and sustained management, advancing long-term conservation and hunting opportunities.

This project reduced hazardous fuels and restored more natural vegetation patterns through mastication and lop-and-scatter treatments on public lands. Creating a diverse mix of plant types and age classes improved forage availability, cover, and overall habitat conditions for mule deer and other wildlife.

Contract fire crews worked with the Forest Service to safely burn piles of woody debris in the Burnt Beaver Project area. These pile burns lower wildfire risk, promote the regrowth of nutritious vegetation, and maintain high-quality habitat for mule deer.

properties.Narrative: This project improved habitat connectivity by converting woven wire and five-strand fencing to wildlife-friendly fencing on public lands. The fencing modifications reduced movement barriers and allowed mule deer and other wildlife to move more safely across the landscape.

properties.Project Name: Highway Tanks / Timber Camp Restoration Project
properties.Public Name: Highway Tanks / Timber Camp Restoration Project
properties.Narrative: This project implemented forest thinning treatments to improve native grasslands and rangeland conditions. By reducing dense tree cover, the project improves growing conditions for grasses and forbs that provide important forage for mule deer and other wildlife, while also lowering hazardous fuel loads. The work was completed in partnership with state and federal agencies to support healthier ecosystems, reduce wildfire risk, and promote long-term landscape resilience.

This project restored ecological conditions across the landscape to improve habitat function and long-term resilience for mule deer and other wildlife. Restoration work enhanced forage, cover, water availability, and habitat connectivity, supporting healthier seasonal habitat use and overall herd health.

This project improved forest health and mule deer habitat on public lands through large-scale forest restoration and biomass removal. Thinning dense timber stands and removing excess woody material helped reduce wildfire risk, improve habitat conditions for the Doyle mule deer herd, and support safer, healthier landscapes for wildlife and public recreation.

This project installed wildlife guzzlers in areas where water availability is limited following recent wildfire impacts on public lands. The new water sources improve habitat conditions for mule deer and other wildlife, while one installation was strategically placed to help reduce deer crossings along a nearby highway and improve wildlife safety.

properties.Project Name: Whaleback Mtn. Post Fire Site Prep
properties.Public Name: Whaleback Mtn. Post Fire Site Prep
properties.Narrative: This project prepared burned areas for reforestation by cutting and piling fire-damaged trees for later burning and future conifer seedling planting on public lands. The work helped accelerate forest recovery, reduce hazardous fuels, improve public safety along nearby road corridors, and support the long-term restoration of wildlife habitat.

This project supported collaborative partnerships focused on improving forest health and watershed conditions on public lands. By helping bring partners together and coordinate restoration planning efforts, the project advances long-term habitat improvement, wildfire resilience, and healthy ecosystems that benefit mule deer and other wildlife.

This project expanded ongoing shaded fuel break treatments through the mastication of small-diameter conifers and overgrown brush on public lands. The work reduced hazardous fuel loads, improved wildfire resilience, and increased forage availability for mule deer and other wildlife.

properties.Project Name: Arnold/Avery Phase 2 Mastication & Hazard Tree Felling
properties.Public Name: Arnold/Avery Phase 2 Mastication & Hazard Tree Felling
properties.Narrative: This project expanded ongoing hazardous fuels reduction efforts by masticating dense vegetation and removing beetle-killed hazard trees near local communities and along the popular Arnold Rim Trail. Reducing dead and overstocked timber improved wildfire resilience, protected recreation access, and stimulated new forage growth and acorn production that benefit mule deer and other wildlife.

The objective of this project was to thin conifers and some brush in conifer plantations that were first planted in the 1960's. Trees were too close together and no sun was reaching the ground, so forage was non-existent. Oak trees that did exist were being choked out by the over stocked conifers as well. By removing many trees and creating space between them, we also reduced competition for the remaining trees allowing them to prosper. The ability for sun to now reach the ground also enabled forbs and grasses to grow adding to more available forage for deer.

This project improved campground infrastructure on public lands by replacing worn and missing picnic tables in heavily used recreation areas. Youth conservation crews helped assemble and install the new tables, supporting outdoor recreation, public land stewardship, and access to important wildlife and hunting landscapes.

This project reduced hazardous fuels on public lands by removing leftover log decks created during previous salvage timber operations. The removed material was repurposed locally as firewood, helping reduce wildfire risk while supporting nearby communities and healthier forest conditions for wildlife.

This project replaced multiple wildlife guzzlers destroyed during the Dixie Fire to restore reliable water sources for mule deer and other wildlife on public lands. Reestablishing these critical water supplies supports post-fire habitat recovery, improves drought resilience, and helps maintain healthy wildlife populations across fire-affected landscapes.

In 2022 and 2023, large-scale sagebrush and bitterbrush plantings were completed on Idaho Department of Lands properties to improve mule deer habitat. These efforts help restore critical shrub communities that provide essential winter forage and cover for deer and other wildlife. The project was implemented in partnership with the Idaho Department of Fish and Game and the Bureau of Land Management to support long-term habitat recovery and resilience.
properties.Partner Name: Idaho Department of Fish and Game

properties.Narrative: This project removed several miles of unnecessary fencing and modified additional fence segments to improve wildlife movement across the landscape. Work was completed with the help of dedicated volunteers and partners, reducing barriers that can cause injury or restrict access to important habitat. These improvements increase landscape connectivity and support safer movement for mule deer and other wildlife.

This project used shrub mowing to introduce a younger age class into an aging shrub stand and stimulate increased understory growth. Creating a mix of shrub ages improves habitat quality by promoting diverse, nutritious forage for mule deer and other wildlife. Work occurred across both Forest Service and private lands, with project locations shown only on public lands to respect private landowner privacy.

This project expanded ongoing pinyon–juniper reduction efforts to reconnect and restore native grassland habitat across the landscape. Continued tree removal improved growing conditions for native grasses and forbs, increasing forage availability, supporting seasonal mule deer movement, and creating more resilient habitat for wildlife.

Following the Mangum Fire, this project rebuilt critical water infrastructure on the Kaibab National Forest. Crews installed new pipeline, replaced multiple water systems serving two canyons, and added a larger 10,000-gallon storage tank. These improvements restore reliable water sources for wildlife and livestock, benefiting mule deer by ensuring consistent access to water throughout the year.

This project completed important environmental and wildlife survey work on public lands in preparation for future mule deer habitat restoration efforts. The surveys help guide upcoming vegetation treatments designed to improve forage availability, forest health, and overall habitat conditions for mule deer and other wildlife.

This project began a multi-phase effort to improve mule deer habitat through hand thinning and pile treatments in overstocked forest stands on public lands. Reducing dense vegetation helps improve forest health, stimulate new understory growth, and create better forage and cover conditions for mule deer and other wildlife.

This project restored deer habitat outside of Quincy, California by reducing excessive understory vegetation and woody debris within a recent fire scar to promote healthier forest conditions. Implemented through a partnership between the Mule Deer Foundation and the Plumas National Forest, the work included biomass removal, mastication, and targeted brush and debris cleanup. These treatments improved forage availability, habitat structure, and movement corridors for mule deer while increasing the landscape’s resilience to future wildfire.

properties.Project Name: Rough Plantation Post Fire Site Prep
properties.Public Name: Rough Plantation Post Fire Site Prep
properties.Narrative: This project reduced hazardous fuels and prepared the landscape for future reforestation through mastication, biomass removal, and the removal of excess woody material. The treatment helps restore healthier forest conditions, improve wildfire resilience, and create better long-term habitat for mule deer and other wildlife.

This project improved critical winter range for the Yosemite deer herd through mechanical thinning and mastication treatments on public lands. Reducing hazardous fuels and opening dense vegetation improved forage availability, habitat conditions, and wildfire resilience within this important seasonal habitat area.

properties.Project Name: Revenant Phase 2-Fuels Reduction & Habitat Improvement in Eldorado N.F.
properties.Public Name: Revenant Phase 2-Fuels Reduction & Habitat Improvement in Eldorado N.F.
properties.Narrative: This project reduced hazardous fuels through mastication and biomass removal to improve wildlife habitat and increase landscape resilience. As the final phase of a larger ongoing restoration effort, the work also helps protect nearby communities from catastrophic wildfire while improving habitat conditions for mule deer and other wildlife.

This project masticated fire-damaged trees and brush following the 2015 King Fire to prepare the landscape for conifer reforestation on public lands. The treatment reduced hazardous fuels, supported forest recovery, and helped accelerate the restoration of healthy wildlife habitat.

This project reduced hazardous fuels and prepared burned areas for reforestation through the mastication of fire-damaged trees and brush on public lands. The treatment helped restore healthier forest conditions, improve wildfire resilience, and support future wildlife habitat recovery.

properties.Project Name: Cedar Camp Hazard Tree Removal & Site Prep
properties.Public Name: Cedar Camp Hazard Tree Removal & Site Prep
properties.Narrative: This project removed burned hazard trees along an important road corridor on public lands to improve public safety and prepare the area for reforestation. The work supports long-term forest recovery and helps restore healthier habitat conditions for mule deer and other wildlife.

properties.Project Name: Eagle Lake Ranger Station Wildlife Guzzler Install
properties.Public Name: Eagle Lake Ranger Station Wildlife Guzzler Install
properties.Narrative: This project installed a wildlife guzzler on public lands to provide a reliable water source for mule deer and other wildlife in an area where water is limited. The placement was strategically selected to help reduce deer crossings along Highway 36 and improve wildlife safety.

Following the Laidlaw Fire, extensive sagebrush and bitterbrush plantings were completed on Bureau of Land Management lands to restore large areas of fire-affected habitat. These plantings are helping re-establish sagebrush communities that provide critical forage and cover for mule deer, elk, pronghorn, and sage-grouse. The project supports long-term post-fire recovery by rebuilding resilient shrub habitats across the landscape.

This project entailed the repair of a water system that was damaged in a recent wildfire and the installation of exclusionary fencing around a natural spring and wet meadow. These efforts will improve livestock grazing management, water availability, and the health of riparian vegetation that provides quality forage and cover for mule deer and other wildlife.

properties.Public Name: South Dakota 2023 Private Lands Habitat Enhancement #1
properties.Narrative: This project entailed the planting of cover crops on previously farmed marginal cropland that was dominated by annual invasive grasses, and the removal of obsolete fencing that posed entanglement hazards to big game. Future project phases will include planting native grasses and forbs to restore grassland conditions that benefit managed livestock grazing, as well as provide forage and cover for mule deer and other wildlife.

This project conducted wildlife, botany, and archaeological surveys to support future aspen restoration planning. The surveys helped guide restoration efforts that improve aspen habitat, benefiting mule deer by enhancing forage diversity, cover, and seasonal habitat use.

properties.Project Name: Strawberry Ridge Watershed Restoration Project FY 23
properties.Public Name: Strawberry Ridge Watershed Restoration Project FY 23
properties.Narrative: This project completed planning and environmental surveys to support forest and wildlife habitat treatments aimed at improving aspen and conifer stand health. The work laid the foundation for future habitat improvements, benefiting mule deer by enhancing forage, cover, and overall forest resilience

This partnered project completed tree stand improvement work in the Ashley National Forest to enhance forest health and resilience. By thinning overcrowded trees and promoting a more diverse understory, the project supports better forage and cover for mule deer, improving the quality of their habitat across the landscape.

Contracted crews completed lop-and-scatter work in dense young lodgepole pine stands to reduce fuel buildup and improve forest health. This treatment opens up the forest floor, encouraging new vegetation growth that provides better forage and movement areas for mule deer.

properties.Project Name: Southeastern Medicine Bow National Forest Native Shrub Restoration
properties.Public Name: Southeastern Medicine Bow National Forest Native Shrub Restoration
properties.Narrative: This project restored shrubland habitat within the Badger and Mullen Fire burn scars through the planting of native shrub species on public lands. Reestablishing native vegetation improves forage, cover, and long-term habitat resilience for mule deer and other wildlife following severe wildfire impacts.

properties.Project Name: West Escudilla PJ Restoration Project (Phase 4 - Dry Lakes)
properties.Public Name: West Escudilla PJ Restoration Project (Phase 4 - Dry Lakes)
properties.Narrative: This project continues a multi-year restoration of historic grassland and savanna habitat through targeted pinyon–juniper reduction on public lands. Maintaining lower tree densities helps sustain native grasses and forbs that provide important forage and seasonal habitat for mule deer and other wildlife while improving overall landscape resilience.

This project supported post-wildfire recovery through cut-and-pile treatments and biomass removal that reduced hazardous debris and prepared the landscape for future reforestation. The work helps restore healthier forest conditions, improve wildfire resilience, and support long-term wildlife habitat recovery.

This project supported post-wildfire forest recovery through the planting of native conifer seedlings on public lands. Reestablishing forest cover helps restore long-term habitat conditions, improve landscape resilience, and provide important thermal and hiding cover for mule deer and other wildlife.

This project improved habitat connectivity for the Modoc Interstate mule deer herd by removing old, abandoned fencing that restricted wildlife movement within an important migration corridor. The work helps mule deer and other wildlife move more freely across the landscape while reducing the risk of wildlife entanglement and injury.

This project supported planning and environmental review work for future habitat restoration projects on public lands. Upcoming treatments will include forest thinning, aspen restoration, and water improvements designed to enhance forage, improve summer range and fawning habitat, and support healthier mule deer populations.

This project supported a coordinated aerial treatment effort to reduce invasive cheatgrass across important shrubland and grassland habitat on public and private lands. Improving native vegetation conditions enhances forage quality, increases habitat resilience to wildfire, and benefits mule deer, bighorn sheep, pronghorn, and other wildlife.

This project improved forest health on public lands through fuels reduction and the removal of dead and dying trees impacted by insects and disease. The work helps reduce wildfire risk, protect important watershed resources that supply downstream water users, and support healthier habitat conditions for mule deer and other wildlife.

MDF partnered with Idaho Department of Fish and Game to plant 42,740 sagebrush, bitterbrush, and rabbitbrush shrubs on the 2024 Paddock Fire. This project is within critical winter range for the Emmett mule deer herd and restored important forage to the landscape.
properties.Partner Name: Idaho Department of Fish and Game

properties.Narrative: MDF in partnership with Idaho Department of Fish and Game, Utah Division of Wildlife Resources, US Fish and Wildlife Service, Bureau of Land Management, and the private landowner modified 11.3 miles of fence to wildlife friendly specifications in a cross-boundary project. This included both state line fence & internal fence on private property. The Idaho Stone Hills mule deer herd's terminal winter range is in UT on the north side of I-84 requiring cross-boundary coordination for habitat improvement.

properties.Project Name: Salmon River Mountains Fuels Reduction and Habitat Improvement Project and Surveys
properties.Public Name: Salmon River Mountains Fuels Reduction and Habitat Improvement Project and Surveys
properties.Narrative: This project reduced dense conifers to improve fire resilience while enhancing habitat conditions for mule deer. Thinning allows grasses and shrubs to grow more vigorously, increasing the quality and availability of forage.

properties.Project Name: RMR NFWF Salmon River Mountains Cheatgrass Treatment
properties.Narrative: This project improved critical mule deer winter range in the Salmon River Mountains through targeted aerial treatment to reduce cheatgrass on important private and state lands near Salmon, Idaho. Implemented through a collaborative effort with conservation partners, the work restored more resilient native plant communities and reduced wildfire risk in a high-value seasonal habitat. By improving winter forage availability and vegetation structure, the project supports stronger mule deer survival and long-term population health across this key landscape.

properties.Project Name: East Deer Lodge Valley Stewardship Project
properties.Public Name: East Deer Lodge Valley Stewardship Project
properties.Narrative: This project selectively hand-thinned hazardous fuel trees to reduce wildfire risk while preserving the structure of healthy forest habitat. By lowering the likelihood of high-severity fires, it protected critical forage and cover areas that mule deer relied on for feeding, shelter, and migration. The result was a more resilient landscape that supported long-term mule deer population health and movement as well as the protection of surrounding communities.

In partnership with the Lolo National Forest, this project completed forest thinning and hazardous fuels reduction treatments on public lands near adjacent private properties. The work improved forest health, reduced wildfire risk, and enhanced habitat conditions for mule deer and other big game species.

In partnership with the Lolo National Forest, this project completed hazardous fuels reduction treatments near local communities within the Superior Ranger District. The work reduced wildfire risk while improving forest health, forage availability, and habitat conditions for mule deer and other big game species.

In partnership with the Lolo National Forest, this project completed hazardous fuels reduction treatments within Hunting District 202 and along the Highway 12 corridor to reduce wildfire risk and improve forest resilience. The work enhanced big game habitat by promoting healthier understory vegetation, increasing forage availability, and improving habitat conditions for mule deer and other wildlife.

MDF volunteers assisted in the removal of old woven wire and barbed-wire fencing that posed entanglement and collision hazards to wildlife. These efforts reduced the risk of big game injury and mortality associated with fencing and helped decrease habitat fragmentation, allowing mule deer and other wildlife to move safely across the landscape.

properties.Project Name: Mule Deer Foundation Sitka Black-tailed Deer Pre/Post Treatment Monitoring in Southeast Alaska
properties.Public Name: Mule Deer Foundation Sitka Black-tailed Deer Pre/Post Treatment Monitoring in Southeast Alaska
properties.Narrative: This project develops a long-term monitoring strategy to evaluate how habitat enhancement projects affect Sitka black-tailed deer and the landscapes they depend on. The results will help guide adaptive management and ensure future restoration work continues to improve habitat quality and deer population resilience.

properties.Project Name: Sitka Black Tailed Deer Habitat Modeling
properties.Public Name: Sitka Black Tailed Deer Habitat Modeling
properties.Narrative: This project uses mapping, data analysis, and local expertise to identify priority habitat areas for Sitka black-tailed deer conservation across Southeast Alaska. The work helps guide future restoration and management decisions that protect important seasonal habitat and support long-term deer population health.
